name: Coriander Chutney
description: Coriander Coconut Chutney or Kothamalli Chutney is a vibrant green South Indian chutney made with fresh coriander leaves, coconut, ginger and green chillies. It is aromatic, mildly spiced and pairs beautifully with idli, dosa, vada, upma and pongal. The fresh herb flavour of coriander makes this chutney a refreshing alternative to plain coconut chutney.

ingredients.name: Coriander leaves (Cilantro)
ingredients.notes: loosely packed, hard stems removed, tender stems okay

steps.instruction: Rinse coriander leaves thoroughly. Remove hard stems — thin tender stems are fine to use.
steps.tip: Remove hard stems of coriander as they can make the chutney bitter. Use leaves and thin tender stems only.

steps.instruction: To a blender add grated coconut, coriander leaves, ginger, green chilli and salt.
Add ¾ to 1 cup water and blend to a smooth consistency.
Transfer the chutney to a bowl. Add lemon juice if desired and mix.
steps.tip: Add a pinch of tamarind for a slight tang if you prefer.

steps.instruction: For tempering, heat 2 tsp oil in a small pan on medium heat.
Add mustard seeds and let them pop. Then add urad dal and cook until it begins to change colour.
Add asafoetida and curry leaves. Stir and turn off heat.
steps.tip: Use a gluten-free asafoetida to make the chutney fully gluten-free, or skip it.

steps.instruction: Pour the tempering over the chutney and mix well.
Serve chilled or at room temperature with idli, dosa, vada, upma or pongal.
steps.tip: This chutney keeps well in the refrigerator for 5 to 7 days.

tips: Remove hard stems of coriander as they can make the chutney bitter. Use leaves and thin tender stems only.
Use a gluten-free asafoetida to make the chutney fully gluten-free, or skip it.
For a spicier chutney, add more green chillies.
This chutney keeps well in the refrigerator for 5 to 7 days.
Frozen grated coconut works perfectly — thaw to room temperature before using.
Add a pinch of tamarind for a slight tang if you prefer.
